mn141:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Bārāṇasī in the Deer Park at Isipatana.
mn141:1.5: “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him.
mn141:2.1: “Monks, near Bārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at Isipatana, the Tathāgata—worthy & rightly self-awakened—set in motion the unexcelled Wheel of Dhamma that cannot be stopped by contemplative or brahman, deva, Māra, or Brahmā or anyone at all in the cosmos:
mn141:2.2: in other words, the declaration, teaching, description, setting-forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of the four noble truths.
mn141:3.1: The declaration, teaching, description, setting-forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of the noble truth of stress. The declaration, teaching, description, setting forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of the noble truth of the origination of stress… the noble truth of the cessation of stress… the noble truth of the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress.
mn141:4.1: Near Bārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at Isipatana, the Tathāgata—worthy & rightly self-awakened—set in motion the unexcelled Wheel of Dhamma that cannot be stopped by contemplative or brahman, deva, Māra, or Brahmā or anyone at all in the cosmos:
mn141:4.2: in other words, the declaration, teaching, description, setting-forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of these four noble truths.
mn141:5.3: Sāriputta & Moggallāna are wise & sympathetic toward the monks who are their companions in the holy life.
mn141:5.4: Like the mother giving birth: That’s Sāriputta.
mn141:5.5: Like the nurse raising a child after it’s born: That’s Moggallāna.
mn141:5.6: Sāriputta trains (others) to the fruit of stream-entry; Moggallāna, to the highest goal.
mn141:5.7: Sāriputta is capable of declaring, teaching, describing, setting forth, revealing, explaining, and making plain the four noble truths in detail.”
mn141:6.1: That is what the Blessed One said.
mn141:6.2: Having said it, he—the One Well-Gone—rose from his seat and entered his dwelling.
mn141:7.1: Then Ven. Sāriputta, not long after the Blessed One had left, addressed the monks,
mn141:7.3: “Yes, friend,” the monks responded to him.
mn141:8.1: “Friends, near Bārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at Isipatana, the Tathāgata—worthy & rightly self-awakened—set in motion the unexcelled Wheel of Dhamma that cannot be stopped by contemplative or brahman, deva, Māra, or Brahmā or anyone at all in the cosmos:
mn141:8.2: in other words, the declaration, teaching, description, setting-forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of the four noble truths.
mn141:9.1: The declaration, teaching, description, setting-forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of the noble truth of stress… the noble truth of the origination of stress… the noble truth of the cessation of stress… the noble truth of the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress. Near Bārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at Isipatana, the Tathāgata—the worthy & rightly self-awakened—set in motion the unexcelled Wheel of Dhamma that cannot be stopped by contemplative or brahman, deva, Māra, or Brahmā or anyone at all in the cosmos: in other words, the declaration, teaching, description, setting-forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of these four noble truths.
mn141:10.1: “Now what, friends, is the noble truth of stress?
mn141:10.2: Birth is stressful, aging is stressful, death is stressful; sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair are stressful; not getting what is wanted is stressful.2 In short, the five clinging-aggregates are stressful.
mn141:11.2: Whatever birth, taking birth, descent, coming-to-be, coming-forth, appearance of aggregates, & acquisition of (sense) spheres of the various beings in this or that group of beings,
mn141:12.2: Whatever aging, decrepitude, brokenness, graying, wrinkling, decline of life-force, weakening of the faculties of the various beings in this or that group of beings,
mn141:13.2: Whatever deceasing, passing away, breaking up, disappearance, dying, death, completion of time, break-up of the aggregates, casting off of the body, interruption in the life faculty of the various beings in this or that group of beings,
mn141:14.2: Whatever sorrow, sorrowing, sadness, inward sorrow, inward sadness of anyone suffering from misfortune, touched by a painful thing,
mn141:15.2: Whatever crying, grieving, lamenting, weeping, wailing, lamentation of anyone suffering from misfortune, touched by a painful thing,
mn141:16.2: Whatever is experienced as bodily pain, bodily discomfort, pain or discomfort born of bodily contact,
mn141:17.2: Whatever is experienced as mental pain, mental discomfort, pain or discomfort born of mental contact,
mn141:18.2: Whatever despair, despondency, desperation of anyone suffering from misfortune, touched by a painful thing,
mn141:19.1: “And what is the stress of not getting what is wanted?
mn141:19.2: In beings subject to birth, the wish arises,
mn141:19.3: ‘O, may we not be subject to birth, and may birth not come to us.’
mn141:19.4: But this is not to be achieved by wishing.
mn141:19.5: This is the stress of not getting what is wanted.
mn141:19.9: sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair, the wish arises,
mn141:19.10: ‘O, may we not be subject to sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair, and may sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair not come to us.’
mn141:19.11: But this is not to be achieved by wishing.
mn141:19.12: This is the stress of not getting what is wanted.
mn141:20.1: “And what are the five clinging-aggregates that, in short, are stressful?
mn141:20.2: The form clinging-aggregate, the feeling clinging-aggregate, the perception clinging-aggregate, the fabrication clinging-aggregate, the consciousness clinging-aggregate:
mn141:20.3: These are called the five clinging-aggregates that, in short, are stressful.
mn141:20.4: “This, friends, is called the noble truth of stress.
mn141:21.1: “And what, friends, is the noble truth of the origination of stress?
mn141:21.2: The craving that makes for further becoming—accompanied by passion & delight, relishing now here & now there—i.e.,
mn141:21.3: craving for sensuality, craving for becoming, craving for non-becoming.
mn141:21.4: “This is called the noble truth of the origination of stress.
mn141:22.1: “And what, friends, is the noble truth of the cessation of stress?
mn141:22.2: The remainderless fading & cessation, renunciation, relinquishment, release, & letting go of that very craving.
mn141:22.3: “This is called the noble truth of the cessation of stress.
mn141:23.1: “And what, friends, is the noble truth of the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress?
mn141:23.2: Just this very noble eightfold path:
mn141:23.3: right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.
mn141:24.2: Knowledge in terms of stress, knowledge in terms of the origination of stress, knowledge in terms of the cessation of stress, knowledge in terms of the way of practice leading to the cessation of stress:
mn141:25.2: The resolve for renunciation, for freedom from ill will, for harmlessness:
mn141:26.2: Abstaining from lying, from divisive speech, from abusive speech, & from idle chatter:
mn141:27.2: Abstaining from taking life, from stealing, & from sexual misconduct:
mn141:28.2: There is the case where a disciple of the noble ones, having abandoned dishonest livelihood, keeps his life going with right livelihood:
mn141:29.2: There is the case where a monk generates desire, endeavors, arouses persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the non-arising of evil, unskillful qualities that have not yet arisen…
mn141:29.3: for the sake of the abandoning of evil, unskillful qualities that have arisen…
mn141:29.4: for the sake of the arising of skillful qualities that have not yet arisen…
mn141:29.5: (and) for the maintenance, non-confusion, increase, plenitude, development, & culmination of skillful qualities that have arisen:
mn141:30.2: There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—putting aside greed & distress with reference to the world.
mn141:30.3: He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves…
mn141:30.5: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—putting aside greed & distress with reference to the world.
mn141:31.2: There is the case where a monk—quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities—enters & remains in the first jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of seclusion, accompanied by directed thought & evaluation.
mn141:31.3: With the stilling of directed thoughts & evaluations, he enters & remains in the second jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of concentration, unification of awareness free from directed thought & evaluation—internal assurance.
mn141:31.4: With the fading of rapture he remains equanimous, mindful, & alert, and senses pleasure with the body. He enters & remains in the third jhāna, of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous & mindful, he has a pleasant abiding.’
mn141:31.5: With the abandoning of pleasure & pain—as with the earlier disappearance of elation & distress—he enters & remains in the fourth jhāna: purity of equanimity & mindfulness, neither pleasure nor pain.
mn141:31.7: “This is called the noble truth of the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress.
mn141:32.1: “Friends, near Bārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at Isipatana, the Tathāgata—the worthy & rightly self-awakened—set in motion the unexcelled Wheel of Dhamma that cannot be stopped by contemplative or brahman, deva, Māra, or Brahmā or anyone at all in the cosmos:
mn141:32.2: in other words, the declaration, teaching, description, setting-forth, revelation, explanation, and making-plain of these four noble truths.”
mn141:32.3: That is what Ven. Sāriputta said.
mn141:32.4: Gratified, the monks delighted in Ven. Sāriputta’s words.
